title: Tensor.requires_grad
text: Tensor.requires_grad is a boolean attribute that indicates whether the tensor is being tracked by autograd for gradient computation. When requires_grad is True, all operations on the tensor are recorded in a computation graph, and calling .backward() on a downstream result will compute gradients with respect to this tensor. When requires_grad is False, the tensor is not part of the computation graph and autograd will not compute gradients for it. By default, tensors created by user code have requires_grad=False. Tensors created as nn.Module parameters have requires_grad=True. To change requires_grad on an existing tensor, use tensor.requires_grad_(True) or tensor.requires_grad_(False). Setting requires_grad to False for specific parameters is the standard way to freeze parts of a model during fine-tuning. If you call loss.backward() and the computation graph does not include any tensor with requires_grad=True, autograd cannot compute gradients and will raise an error. This commonly happens when model parameters are accidentally detached from the graph, or when a tensor was created without requires_grad=True. Note that requires_grad=False does not freeze the entire model automatically. It only affects the specific tensor it is set on. To freeze all parameters of a module, you must iterate over model.parameters() and set requires_grad=False on each one individually.

title: Tensor.detach
text: Tensor.detach() returns a new tensor that shares the same underlying data but is detached from the computation graph. The detached tensor has requires_grad=False and will not have a grad_fn, even if the original tensor was part of a gradient computation. This is a per-tensor operation: it creates a new tensor object that is separated from autograd tracking while keeping the data. The key difference between detach() and torch.no_grad() is their scope and mechanism. detach() operates on a single tensor and removes it from the graph permanently. torch.no_grad() is a context manager that temporarily disables gradient tracking for all operations within its scope. After exiting the torch.no_grad() context, gradient tracking resumes for subsequent operations. Use detach() when you need a specific tensor's value without maintaining its connection to the computation graph, for example when storing intermediate activations for logging, or when passing a value to a loss function that should not receive gradients through that path. Use torch.no_grad() when you want to run an entire block of operations without gradient tracking, such as during model evaluation or inference. detach() does not copy the data. The detached tensor shares storage with the original. Modifying the detached tensor will affect the original tensor's data.

title: Tensor.grad
text: Tensor.grad stores the gradient computed by autograd for a leaf tensor with requires_grad=True. After calling loss.backward(), the .grad attribute of each leaf tensor that participated in the computation will contain the gradient of the loss with respect to that tensor. Gradients in PyTorch accumulate by default. Each call to loss.backward() adds the newly computed gradient to the existing .grad value instead of replacing it. This means that if you call backward() multiple times without clearing gradients, the .grad attribute will contain the sum of all computed gradients. This accumulation behavior is intentional and useful for gradient accumulation across micro-batches, but it requires explicitly zeroing gradients before each standard training step. To clear accumulated gradients, call optimizer.zero_grad() at the beginning of each training iteration, before computing the forward pass and calling backward(). Without zeroing, gradients from previous steps will contaminate the current step's update, leading to incorrect parameter updates. After backward(), only leaf tensors (those created directly by the user, not as a result of operations) will have their .grad populated. Intermediate tensors do not retain gradients by default unless retain_grad() was called on them before the backward pass.
